titleStmt.title: Postcard from George D. Roche to Nancy McCarthy, 8 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: George D. Roche

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
In this postcard to Nancy McCarthy, her uncle George D. Roche inquires if they had much snow and tells her that they have had quite a lot of snow and "beaucoup snowballing!”
Nancy McCarthy was a well-practiced letter-writer, and her collection of correspondence spans eighty years. This postcard is one of seven included in the Letters of 1916 collection written to Nancy by her uncle, George D. Roche, on active service with the 7th London regiment during World War I.

msIdentifier.repository: UCC Library Special Collections and Archives

correspAction.placeName: The Convent, Mountrath, Queen's County, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
THÉROUANNE (P.-de-C)
(Ville fortifiee detruite par Charles.Quint
en 1553).
Statue de St Martin le célèbre
Général-Évêque qui coupà
son manteau avec son sabre
pour en donner une moitié
à un pauvre
CARTE POSTALE Miss Nancy McCarthy
Brigidine Convent
Mountrath
Queen's Co.
Ireland 8/3/16 Dear Nancy How are Eily and yourself
Have you much snow? We
have had quite a lot and
beaucoup snowballing!
The country looks lovely this
morning under its white
garment.
Love to you both from
Uncle George
